planting.note: Grey-green leaves edged in creamy white; plant in spring in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant where its variegation lights up a shaded border, giving it shelter from harsh afternoon sun. Trim after flowering to keep it tidy, removing any all-green shoots that appear. Clusters of white flowers open in late spring against the marbled cream-and-grey foliage.

planting.note: New growth flushes bright yellow; plant in full sun for best color.

directions: Plant in full sun so the young leaves emerge a bright gold before aging to green. Light shearing through the season keeps a steady supply of colorful new growth. White flower clusters open in late spring, though the golden foliage is the main attraction.

planting.note: A compact non-flowering gold form; plant in full sun for vivid color.

directions: Plant in full sun, where the leaves turn their brightest chartreuse to gold, and space for a low hedge. Shear it freely into mounds or borders, since this selection does not flower or set seed. It is grown purely for the year-round glow of its foliage rather than any bloom.

planting.note: Small leaves edged in creamy white; plant in spring or fall.

directions: Plant at nursery depth and give it partial shade to keep the cream margins fresh. Remove any plain green shoots promptly so the variegation does not revert. Loose panicles of scented white flowers appear in late spring on the arching stems.

planting.note: New leaves flushed pink over cream and green; plant in spring in a bright spot.

directions: Plant in a bright but sheltered position so the young pink-tinged foliage colors well. Cut out any reverted green stems and trim lightly to encourage fresh, colorful growth. Fragrant white flower clusters open in late spring on the graceful branches.

planting.note: Bright yellow-margined leaves; plant in sun for strongest color.

directions: Plant in full sun for the boldest gold-edged leaves and space closely for a colorful hedge. Shear two or three times a season to keep dense, well-colored growth, cutting out any all-green reverting shoots. Small white flowers appear in summer on unclipped stems, but the variegated foliage is the main feature.

planting.note: Leaves edged in creamy white; plant in spring or fall in ordinary soil.

directions: Plant closely for a bright hedge and firm the soil around the roots after setting each plant. Clip regularly to keep it dense, removing plain green shoots to preserve the creamy margins. Small white flowers open in summer on stems left unsheared.

planting.note: A compact low form for small hedges; plant in spring or fall.

directions: Space plants closely for a low edging hedge and trim after planting to thicken the base. Shear once or twice a season to keep the neat, rounded outline, as it stays naturally compact. Small white flower clusters open in early summer on unclipped growth.

planting.note: Selected for cold and heat tolerance and good leaf retention; plant spring or fall.

directions: Set plants in a row and cut back the tops after planting to promote low branching. Shear regularly for a formal hedge that holds its glossy leaves later into fall than most common privets. Clusters of white flowers open in early summer on stems left unpruned.

planting.note: Large leaves marked with deep yellow and cream; plant in spring in a bright spot.

directions: Give this large variegated tree a sunny, sheltered site with room to spread. Remove any all-green shoots as they appear to keep the bold golden markings. Sprays of white flowers open in summer, ripening to dark berries by autumn.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring once hard frosts have passed.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil with the root ball level to the surface and water deeply while roots establish. This vigorous evergreen tolerates coastal wind and salt spray, making it a reliable seaside hedge. Shear lightly after the main flush of tubular red flowers, which appear from early summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring so the shrub roots in before its first winter.

directions: Give this robust form room to spread and plant it where its glossy leaves can screen a boundary. It is among the most salt-tolerant escallonias and thrives in exposed coastal gardens. Trim back after the deep rose-red summer flowers fade to keep the growth dense.

planting.note: Plant against a sheltered warm wall in cooler regions.

directions: Site this large shrub in a warm, sheltered spot with free-draining soil, as it is less cold-hardy than the red-flowered kinds. Panicles of pure white flowers open in late summer and autumn and draw butterflies and bees. Prune only to shape, ideally in spring after the worst frosts have passed.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sheltered border.

directions: Choose a spot with fertile, well-drained soil and full sun for the best flowering. Clusters of rose-pink blooms cover the rounded bush through summer. Lightly clip after flowering to maintain a tidy dome-shaped outline.

planting.note: The hardiest species; plant in spring in most temperate gardens.

directions: Plant this deciduous, cold-tolerant species in an open sunny position with good drainage. Its slender arching branches carry small white flowers in early to midsummer. Thin out older stems at the base in late winter to encourage fresh growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring where it has room to reach full size.

directions: Give this tall species a deep, moisture-retentive soil that still drains freely. Erect panicles of white flowers appear in midsummer above glossy foliage. Prune after flowering to control its considerable height and spread.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, free-draining spot.

directions: Plant in full sun where the resinous, aromatic foliage releases a pungent scent when brushed. Loose sprays of white flowers open through summer. Trim lightly to shape after the blooms fade.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm border with shelter from cold wind.

directions: Set in fertile, well-drained ground and shelter it from harsh winter winds. Fragrant pale pink to white flowers cover the bush in summer. Shape lightly after flowering rather than hard-pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a warm wall in colder areas.

directions: Give this large hybrid room to develop its upright, glossy-leaved framework. Fragrant pure white flowers open in late summer and the dark foliage often flushes bronze in cold weather. Prune only to remove wayward shoots after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the arching stems can cascade freely.

directions: Plant this graceful hybrid where its long arching branches have room to sweep outward. Rosy-carmine flowers wreathe the stems from early to midsummer. Prune out a few of the oldest stems after flowering to renew the fountain-like shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny border or as a low hedge.

directions: Plant this popular compact variety in well-drained soil and full sun for the heaviest bloom. Soft pink-and-white flowers reminiscent of apple blossom cover the bush through summer. Clip after the main flush to keep it neat and encourage a repeat show.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a fast-growing informal hedge.

directions: Use this vigorous cultivar as an informal flowering hedge in fertile, well-drained soil. Arching branches carry masses of pale pink buds that open near-white in summer. Trim after flowering to maintain a dense screen.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny sheltered position.

directions: Plant this sturdy, compact cultivar in full sun with good drainage. Large rose-red flowers stand out against thick glossy foliage all summer. Shear lightly after the main bloom to hold its rounded form.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, well-drained border.

directions: Set this free-flowering cultivar in full sun for the richest color. Rose-crimson blooms appear over a long summer season on arching stems. Prune lightly after flowering to keep it compact.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny spot with shelter.

directions: Plant this bushy, upright cultivar in fertile, free-draining soil. Bright rose-pink flowers cover the compact framework through summer. Trim after flowering to keep growth tidy and dense.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the strongest flower color.

directions: Plant in a sunny, sheltered position with good drainage. Large, deep rose-red flowers hang from gracefully arching branches all summer. Shape after flowering to control its vigor.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny border or low hedge.

directions: Give this compact, glossy-leaved cultivar full sun and free-draining soil. Broad rosy-red flowers open over a long summer period. Clip lightly after the main flush to keep it bushy.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its size can be accommodated.

directions: Plant this tall, vigorous cultivar as a specimen or robust hedge in well-drained soil. Crimson flowers stud the large glossy leaves through summer, and the foliage is aromatic when crushed. Prune after flowering to restrain its considerable spread.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a row for a tall formal hedge.

directions: This upright, fast cultivar makes an excellent tall hedge; space plants evenly along the line. Crimson flowers appear from summer into autumn against dark glossy foliage. Trim twice a year to maintain a clean vertical face.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sheltered border.

directions: Plant this compact cultivar in full sun and free-draining soil. Clear peachy-pink flowers cover the bush all summer and attract pollinators. Shear lightly after flowering to keep the rounded habit.

planting.note: Plant in spring; one of the hardier named cultivars.

directions: Plant this relatively hardy cultivar in well-drained soil and full sun. Pale apple-blossom pink flowers cover the arching stems in summer. Prune after flowering to renew growth and hold its shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low informal hedge or foundation shrub.

directions: Widely grown in mild-climate gardens, this compact cultivar thrives in full sun and drains-freely soil. Pink flowers appear over a very long season from spring into autumn. Shear after the heaviest flush to keep it dense and encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low hedge or mass planting.

directions: This low, spreading cultivar is ideal for edging and small hedges in mild climates. Rosy-red flowers dot the dense foliage through much of the growing season. Trim lightly to maintain its naturally compact mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny spot as a compact hedge.

directions: Plant this dense, medium-sized cultivar in full sun and well-drained ground. Deep red tubular flowers appear against small glossy leaves from summer into fall. Clip after flowering to keep it neat and full.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its golden foliage lights up a border.

directions: Grow this golden-leaved cultivar in full sun to keep the bright foliage color, giving light afternoon shade in hot climates to prevent scorch. Rose-pink flowers contrast with the yellow-green leaves in summer. Trim lightly to shape and remove any plain green reverting shoots.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the best variegation.

directions: Plant this variegated cultivar in a sunny position with free-draining soil to bring out its gold-margined leaves. Pink flowers appear in summer above the bright foliage. Prune out any all-green shoots promptly to preserve the variegation.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front of a border or in a rock garden.

directions: This naturally dwarf form suits small gardens, containers, and low edging in full sun. Crimson flowers appear on the compact mound through summer. Remove any tall, reverted shoots that arise so the plant stays low.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny border or patio container.

directions: Plant this compact, long-flowering cultivar in full sun with good drainage. Bright pink blooms are carried over an extended season from early summer into autumn. Clip after the main flush to maintain a tidy dome and prompt more flowers.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a coastal hedge or specimen.

directions: Plant this vigorous cultivar in well-drained soil where it can serve as a wind-tolerant hedge. Bright red flowers appear against dense glossy leaves through summer. Trim after flowering to keep a full, even screen.

planting.note: Set container plants out in spring after hard frost passes, spacing well for the mature spread.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at the same depth it grew in the pot and water regularly through the first season to establish roots. Prune lightly after flowering to shape, since berries form on older wood. Clouds of white flowers open in late spring and give way to dense scarlet berries that hold into winter.

planting.note: Plant against a warm wall or as a freestanding hedge in spring.

directions: Dig a hole twice the width of the rootball, set the plant level with the surrounding soil, and firm it in well. This vigorous old selection trains readily on wires against a wall, so tie in new growth as it lengthens. Expect white spring bloom followed by heavy orange-red fruit that persists through the cold months.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant selection best planted in spring in northern gardens.

directions: Place in a sunny, sheltered spot with free-draining soil and mulch the root zone after planting. Its extra hardiness suits it to exposed sites where other firethorns struggle. Orange-red berries follow the early-summer flowers and color up by autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its arching branches have room to spread.

directions: Set the shrub in a hole level with its rootball and backfill with the native soil, watering deeply to settle it. Thorny stems make it a good barrier plant, so site it away from paths. Small white flowers give way to abundant orange-red berries in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a bright yellow-berried contrast to red forms.

directions: Choose a sunny position with good drainage and plant at the original soil depth. Prune after the summer flowers if you need to control size, being careful to leave fruiting spurs. This form ripens clear yellow berries instead of red, holding them well into winter.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a warm, sunny site with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained soil and avoid overwatering once the shrub is settled. Its narrow gray-green leaves and dense thorns make a tough, drought-hardy barrier. Orange-yellow berries follow the white flowers and can persist right through winter.

planting.note: An unusually cold-hardy selection for colder-zone gardens; plant in spring.

directions: Site it in full sun with excellent drainage and mulch the base to protect roots in its first winters. Bred for extra hardiness, it withstands colder regions than most firethorns. Bright orange berries follow early-summer bloom and feed birds through winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in mild-winter regions where it thrives.

directions: Plant in a sunny spot with free-draining soil and give it room to reach its full spread. This tender-ish species is popular in warm southern gardens and takes well to espalier. Red berries ripen after the white spring flowers and stay colorful into winter.

planting.note: A low, spreading form for banks and groundcover; plant in spring.

directions: Space plants about three feet apart so they knit together into a low mat, and plant level with the soil. This prostrate selection hugs the ground and covers slopes with little pruning. Red berries follow the white flowers and brighten the low mounds through fall and winter.

planting.note: A compact, low-growing form for foreground plantings; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and water until established, then reduce watering. Its low, wide habit suits it to mass plantings and low informal hedges. Orange-red fruit follows the spring flowers and persists into the cooler months.

planting.note: Prized for late-ripening berries; plant in spring in mild regions.

directions: Set the plant at its original depth in well-drained soil and water regularly the first summer. This selection is valued for holding its color late, ripening berries after many others have colored. Deep red fruit follows the white flowers and lasts well into winter.

planting.note: A white-variegated form; plant in spring with a little afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil where it gets bright light but some shelter from scorching afternoon sun. Its cream-edged leaves flush pink in cold weather, adding winter interest beyond the fruit. Red berries follow modest white flowers and set off the variegated foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; widely used for hedges in warm regions.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained ground and space closely if you want a solid hedge. Glossy evergreen leaves and dense thorns make it a strong screen or barrier. Clusters of white flowers are followed by long-lasting red berries.

planting.note: A vigorous large-fruited selection; plant in spring.

directions: Dig a generous hole, set the plant level with the soil surface, and firm the roots in place. This robust selection carries large clusters of dark red berries on strong upright growth. It makes a bold espalier or screen and holds its fruit late into winter.

planting.note: A reliable wall shrub; plant in spring against a fence or wall.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in sun or light shade and tie the growth to supports if training on a wall. Its smaller leaves and tidy habit suit it to formal training. White flowers in early summer are followed by reddish-orange berries.

planting.note: A bright yellow-berried form; plant in spring on a wall or as a hedge.

directions: Set the plant level with the soil and water regularly until it is established. This form ripens clear yellow berries that stand out against the dark evergreen leaves. It trains neatly against walls and holds its fruit through much of winter.

planting.note: A tall, vigorous species for large walls; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained soil and allow plenty of room, as it can grow tall and wide. Its near-rounded leaves stay glossy through winter on strong upright stems. Large crops of scarlet berries follow the white summer flowers and persist for months.

planting.note: A yellow-fruited form of the tall Gibbs firethorn; plant in spring.

directions: Give this large shrub a sunny wall with well-drained soil and tie in leading shoots as they grow. It produces heavy clusters of golden-yellow berries on tall, arching branches. The fruit ripens after the early-summer flowers and lasts deep into winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in mild regions with free-draining soil.

directions: Plant in a sheltered sunny position in well-drained soil and water through the first summer. This Himalayan species carries finely toothed leaves and thorny arching stems. White flower clusters give way to orange-red berries in autumn.

planting.note: A disease-resistant hybrid; plant in spring for an early, heavy berry set.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and prune after flowering only as needed to shape. This vigorous hybrid resists scab and fireblight better than older kinds and fruits early and heavily. Bright orange-red berries follow the white flowers and hold color from fall into winter.

planting.note: A compact, disease-resistant hybrid; plant in spring.

directions: Set out in a sunny, well-drained site and water until established. This mounded hybrid stays lower and broader than many firethorns and shows good disease resistance. Orange-red berries follow the spring bloom and persist well through the season.

planting.note: A compact hybrid with dense berries; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and give it room to spread into a low mound. This disease-resistant hybrid stays compact and produces tight clusters of bright red berries. Fruit follows the white flowers and colors up by autumn.

planting.note: An upright, columnar hybrid good for narrow spaces; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained spot and use its narrow upright habit for tight corners or screening. This hardy, disease-resistant hybrid grows taller than wide with strong vertical stems. Yellow-orange berries follow the white flowers and hold through winter.

planting.note: A low, spreading disease-resistant hybrid; plant in spring.

directions: Set the plant level with the soil in a sunny, freely draining spot and water to establish. Its low, spreading form works well for banks and informal barriers. Orange-red berries follow the spring flowers and remain colorful into winter.

planting.note: An early-ripening, disease-resistant hybrid; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and prune lightly after bloom to maintain shape. This broad, spreading hybrid resists scab and fireblight and ripens its fruit early. Yellow-orange berries follow the white flowers and last well into the cold months.

planting.note: A hybrid noted for berries that shift from orange to red; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained spot and water regularly through the first season. Its small leaves and cascading branches carry heavy crops of berries that ripen from orange to red. The colorful fruit follows the white spring flowers and holds into winter.

planting.note: A low, wide-spreading disease-resistant hybrid; plant in spring.

directions: Space plants a few feet apart and set them level with the soil so they spread into a low cover. This flat-growing hybrid resists disease and works well over banks and walls. Orange-red berries follow the white flowers and persist through fall and winter.

planting.note: A dense, rounded hybrid popular for hedging; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in sun or light shade in well-drained soil and clip after flowering if used as a hedge. This compact, bushy hybrid holds a neat rounded shape with reliable flowering and fruit. Bright red berries follow abundant white flowers and last well into winter.

planting.note: A vigorous hybrid with rich orange berries; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil against a wall or in the open and tie in stems if training. This popular hybrid produces heavy clusters of deep orange berries on strong upright growth. The fruit follows the white summer flowers and holds its color through much of winter.

planting.note: A golden-berried hybrid; plant in spring for bright winter color.

directions: Set the plant in a sunny, well-drained spot and water until established. This hybrid ripens masses of golden-yellow berries that stand out against the dark leaves. Fruit follows the white flowers and stays bright through the winter months.

planting.note: A vigorous orange-gold berried hybrid; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in sun or light shade and prune after bloom only as needed. This vigorous hybrid carries large clusters of orange-gold berries on arching stems. The fruit follows the white flowers and holds well into winter.

planting.note: A hybrid with deep orange berries; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained position and water regularly the first year. This upright hybrid bears generous clusters of rich orange berries on strong branches. Fruit ripens after the white flowers and remains showy into winter.

planting.note: A rounded, mounding hybrid with yellow berries; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and allow room for its broad, mounding shape. This hybrid forms a dense dome and covers itself in golden-yellow berries. The fruit follows the white flowers and brightens the plant through winter.

planting.note: An upright hybrid that reddens its stems in winter; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained spot and use its upright form for narrow beds or screening. Young stems take on a reddish cast that adds interest in the cold season. Deep red berries follow the white flowers and ripen early in autumn.

planting.note: A pink-tinged variegated form; plant in spring with some shelter.

directions: Plant in bright light with shelter from harsh afternoon sun and give it well-drained soil. Its cream-marbled leaves flush pink in cold weather, making it grown as much for foliage as fruit. Modest red berries follow the white flowers and set off the variegation.

planting.note: A compact yellow-berried selection for small gardens; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and prune lightly to keep it compact. This smaller-growing form suits tight spaces and containers and carries clear yellow berries. The fruit follows the white flowers and holds its color into winter.

planting.note: A disease-resistant French selection; plant in spring for hedging.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in sun or light shade and space evenly for a hedge. This selection was bred for strong resistance to scab and fireblight and needs little spraying. Bright orange berries follow the white flowers and last well into winter.

planting.note: A disease-resistant red-berried French selection; plant in spring.

directions: Set out in well-drained soil in a sunny spot and water until established. Like others in its series, it resists scab and fireblight and stays healthy with little care. Deep red berries follow the white flowers and hold through the winter.

planting.note: A disease-resistant yellow-berried French selection; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and trim after flowering if kept as a hedge. This selection combines good disease resistance with clear yellow fruit. The berries follow the white flowers and brighten the shrub into winter.

planting.note: A true dwarf for containers and small beds; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil or a large pot in full sun and prune lightly to keep it tidy. This compact selection stays small enough for containers while still fruiting freely. Bright red berries follow the white flowers and last into winter.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Plant with the crown level with the surrounding soil and train the stiff branches over a wall or low bank where the herringbone pattern shows. Water through the first season, then treat it as drought-tolerant. Small pink-tinged flowers open in late spring and give way to bright red berries by autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a slope or bank where the trailing stems can root as they spread.

directions: Space plants so the prostrate stems knit together into a weed-smothering carpet, setting each one at the depth it grew in its pot. Peg down long shoots to speed coverage on erosion-prone ground. White flowers stud the evergreen mat in early summer and mature into red berries that persist into winter.

planting.note: Set out in spring in full sun for the heaviest berry display.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil and mulch lightly to keep roots cool while the low mound fills in. Little pruning is needed beyond removing any stray upright shoots. Clusters of white flowers in early summer ripen to abundant coral-orange berries that color best in an open, sunny spot.

planting.note: Plant in spring for fast groundcovering on slopes and banks.

directions: Set plants a couple of feet apart and let the vigorous flat stems race outward to cover ground quickly. Keep new plantings watered until established, then only in drought. White early-summer flowers are followed by red fruit on a dense evergreen mat.

planting.note: Plant in spring where a very flat, tight carpet is wanted.

directions: Plant this ground-hugging selection at the pot's original depth and space closely for a smooth low sheet over rock gardens or wall tops. It needs almost no pruning. Small white flowers appear in early summer, followed by scattered red berries on the near-flat mat.

planting.note: Plant in spring; foliage takes on orange tints in autumn.

directions: Set out plants in well-drained ground and allow the arching stems to spread into a low mound. Trim only to keep it in bounds. White summer flowers give red berries, and the small glossy leaves flush orange-red before winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered site to protect evergreen foliage from cold winds.

directions: Give this large arching shrub room to spread and plant it out of harsh winter wind. Prune after fruiting only to shape or remove damaged wood. Flat heads of white flowers open in early summer and ripen into heavy trusses of red berries carried well into winter on narrow willow-like leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a tough evergreen groundcover for banks.

directions: Space plants to let the low branches spread and root into a dense weed-suppressing cover. Water the first year while it establishes. White flowers in early summer are followed by red berries above glossy dark leaves that redden in cold weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a dwarf, low-mounding evergreen carpet.

directions: Plant this compact selection at pot depth and let the fine-textured stems weave together low to the ground. Minimal pruning keeps it neat. Tiny white summer flowers turn to small red berries among narrow evergreen leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring on slopes for a spreading evergreen mat.

directions: Set plants a few feet apart and allow the arching branches to trail and root down a bank. Once rooted it tolerates dry spells well. White flowers in early summer are followed by a heavy fall crop of orange-red berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring as an evergreen hedge or specimen in a mild site.

directions: Plant this dense evergreen at hedge spacing and clip lightly after fruiting if a formal shape is wanted. It takes hard pruning to renovate an overgrown plant. Broad clusters of creamy white flowers open in early summer and mature to long-lasting red berries above leathery veined leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring for an arching hedge or screen.

directions: Give this graceful shrub room for its arching habit and plant it where the grey-green foliage catches the light. Prune only to thin or shape after the berries fade. Pink-flushed white flowers in early summer become orange-red berries, and the leaves are silvery beneath.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a rock garden or over a wall.

directions: Plant into gritty, well-drained soil and let the stiff mounding branches drape over rocks or a low wall. It needs little care once settled in. Small white flowers dot the tiny glossy leaves in late spring, giving rose-red berries in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a spreading low shrub for slopes and foundations.

directions: Set plants at the depth they grew and allow the stiff spreading branches to fill in a bank or bed edge. Prune only to remove damage or crossing stems. Pink flowers in late spring are followed by large cranberry-red berries, and the small leaves turn bronze-red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rock gardens or troughs where a tiny mound suits.

directions: Plant this diminutive selection in well-drained soil and let it build a tight low cushion needing no pruning. It rarely flowers or fruits heavily, grown mainly for its neat form and glossy foliage that colors in fall. Give it a spot at the front of a bed or in a trough where its small scale reads.

planting.note: Plant in spring as an informal hedge or screen.

directions: Space plants for an informal barrier and let the wide-angled branches build a dense twiggy shrub. It withstands hard pruning if size must be controlled. Small pink flowers in late spring give egg-shaped red berries, and the foliage turns orange to red before dropping.

planting.note: Plant in spring over walls or as a low creeping cover.

directions: Plant at the crown level and let the low branches creep and root as they hug the ground or drape over stone. Little pruning is needed. Pinkish flowers in late spring yield bright red berries, and the crinkled leaves flush red in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in troughs or rock gardens for a very slow low mat.

directions: Plant this slow, dense dwarf in gritty soil where its tight tiered branching can be admired up close. It needs no routine pruning. It seldom flowers, grown chiefly for the neat congested form and small leaves that redden in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a rock garden or over a low wall.

directions: Plant into sharply drained soil and let the congested stems form a slow evergreen hummock that hugs rocks. It rarely needs cutting back. Small pinkish-white flowers in early summer become red berries among tiny blue-green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring for an upright hedge or screen.

directions: Set plants closer together for a clipped hedge or wider for a natural screen, using this stiffly upright grower. It tolerates regular shearing. White flowers in early summer are followed by showy orange-red berries on erect branches.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a large specimen with room to arch.

directions: Give this big arching shrub open space so its cascading spring bloom shows to advantage. Prune only to thin old wood after fruiting. Unusually showy clusters of white flowers cover the branches in late spring, followed by red berries and blue-green foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a large shrub for autumn berry and leaf color.

directions: Plant this vigorous shrub where its arching stems and puckered leaves have room to develop. Thin crowded branches after fruiting if needed. Pink-white flowers in early summer give large drooping clusters of red berries, and the deeply veined foliage turns red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a large specimen or small standard tree.

directions: Give this near-tree-sized selection plenty of room, or train it up on a single stem as a small standard. Prune to shape while young. Broad white flower heads in early summer ripen into massive drooping bunches of red berries that weigh the branches down in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a large arching specimen shrub.

directions: Set this vigorous hybrid where its spreading arching form has space to develop. Prune only to remove damaged or crossing wood. Clusters of white flowers open in early summer and give heavy trusses of red berries carried into winter on long leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring for its unusual yellow berries on a large shrub.

directions: Give this large spreading hybrid open space and let the branches arch outward. Prune lightly after fruiting to shape. White summer flowers mature into distinctive creamy-yellow berries that hang in heavy clusters through autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a mounding evergreen for banks or borders.

directions: Plant this dense evergreen where its arching stems can build a broad mound, ideal on a slope. It needs little pruning beyond tidying. Masses of small white flowers smother the plant in early summer and turn to heavy bright red berries that persist through winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring for fast, wide groundcover on banks.

directions: Space plants generously and let the vigorous arching stems cover ground quickly and root as they go. Shear back if it overreaches its bounds. White flowers in early summer give sparse coral-red berries above glossy evergreen leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a very flat, dense evergreen carpet.

directions: Plant this ground-hugging selection at pot depth and space so the flat stems knit into a tight low sheet over banks and wall tops. It needs virtually no pruning. Small white flowers in early summer give red berries on a near-prostrate evergreen mat.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a hardy hedge for cold regions.

directions: Set plants at close spacing for a clipped hedge, choosing this very cold-hardy species for exposed sites. It tolerates repeated shearing well. Small pinkish-white flowers in late spring give black berries, and the pointed leaves turn red-purple in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a well-drained, sunny spot.

directions: Plant into free-draining soil where this compact upright species suits a rock garden or mixed border. Little pruning is required. Nodding pink-white flowers in late spring give bright red berries above rounded grey-green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring as an arching specimen or informal hedge.

directions: Give this graceful shrub room for its arching stems and grey-green, silver-backed leaves. Prune only to shape after the berries fade. White flowers in early summer become abundant orange-red berries in generous clusters.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a graceful arching medium shrub.

directions: Plant where the arching branches and rounded grey-green leaves have room to spread. Thin old stems after fruiting if the plant grows congested. Pink-tinged flowers in late spring give large orange-red berries on gracefully curving branches.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the cream-edged foliage brightens a wall or bank.

directions: Plant this slow variegated form in a spot with some shelter and remove any all-green shoots that appear. The herringbone branches spread low over walls and banks. Tiny leaves edged in cream flush pink and red in autumn, and small pink flowers give a light crop of red berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a mild-winter site as an evergreen screen.

directions: Plant this vigorous evergreen where mild winters let it keep its blue-green foliage, giving it room to arch. Prune after fruiting to shape. White flowers open in early summer, later than many cotoneasters, and ripen into red berries in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a large specimen shrub.

directions: Give this tall shrub open space for its broad spreading habit and large leaves. Thin crowded wood after fruiting. Pinkish-white flowers in early summer give purple-black berries in heavy clusters that draw birds in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low arching cover for banks and walls.

directions: Plant this low spreading species where its arching tiered branches can drape over a wall or bank. It needs little care once established. Pink flowers in late spring give unusually large red berries, and the leaves color well before dropping.

planting.note: Set out container plants in spring or early fall so roots settle before hard weather.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at the same depth the shrub grew in its pot and mulch to hold even moisture. Shear lightly after each flush to keep the brilliant red new growth coming, and avoid wetting the foliage to reduce leaf-spot disease. Flat clusters of small white flowers open in spring on established plants.

planting.note: Best planted in spring or autumn as a specimen or clipped hedge.

directions: Dig a hole twice the width of the rootball in fertile, freely draining ground and firm the soil around the roots. Trim two or three times a season to trigger the glossy scarlet young leaves this selection is prized for. Small white flower heads appear in mid to late spring where growth is left unpruned.

planting.note: Compact enough for containers, low hedges, and small borders; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in a sunny spot with sharp drainage and keep the crown level with the surrounding soil. Pinch back the tips through the growing season to hold its neat mounded shape and refresh the bright red foliage. Occasional white flower clusters may show in spring but the plant is grown mainly for its leaf color.

planting.note: A vigorous, upright selection well suited to tall screens; plant spring or fall.

directions: Space plants for a screen and set each at its original planting depth in well-drained ground. This robust selection tolerates hard pruning, so cut back after spring flowering to keep it dense and to spur coppery new leaves. Clusters of creamy white blooms open in spring on unclipped stems.

planting.note: A French selection with large leaves; plant in spring or early autumn.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained soil and water regularly through the first year to establish deep roots. The broad young leaves emerge fiery red before fading to green, so light shearing prolongs the display. Flat sprays of white flowers can appear in spring where stems are not trimmed.

planting.note: Grown for cream-edged leaves flushed pink; plant spring in a sheltered spot.

directions: Choose a site with morning sun and shelter from cold wind, and plant in freely draining soil. New growth opens bright pink and red over cream-margined leaves, so trim lightly to encourage fresh flushes. Small white flowers may open in spring on established plants.

planting.note: A compact, twisted-leaf form good for pots; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil and keep the base mulched to conserve moisture. The wavy, curled leaves emerge red and mature to deep green, giving a dense rounded outline with minimal pruning. Clusters of white flowers may appear in spring.

planting.note: Creamy-margined foliage brightens shaded corners; plant in spring.

directions: Set into well-drained, fertile soil and shelter from harsh afternoon sun to protect the pale leaf margins. New shoots are red and cream, so light trimming keeps the variegation bright and the plant bushy. White spring flower clusters form on stems left unpruned.

planting.note: An improved red-tip valued for hedging; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and space evenly for a uniform screen. Repeated light shearing forces the strong red leaf color for which this selection is grown, while good air circulation limits leaf spot. Flat white flower heads open in spring on unpruned wood.

planting.note: A slower, more compact form with orange-tinted new growth; plant in spring.

directions: Plant at soil level in a sunny, well-drained bed and mulch to keep roots cool. The bronze-orange young leaves mature to green, and its naturally tidy habit needs less shearing than taller red tips. White flower clusters may appear in spring.

planting.note: Grown for pink, cream and green marbled leaves; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil with shelter from strong midday sun to keep the variegation crisp. The toothed leaves emerge splashed pink and cream, so trim lightly to keep the colorful new growth coming. Small white flowers may open in spring on mature plants.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring, or sow stratified seed in fall.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in sun to light shade and water steadily while young. The smooth young leaves emerge bronzy red and mature to glossy green, and the shrub responds well to shaping. Clusters of white flowers open in spring, followed by red berries.

planting.note: Selected for especially bright red new leaves; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in freely draining soil in full sun to bring out the vivid coppery-red spring growth. Trim after flowering to encourage a fresh flush of colored leaves and a compact habit. White flower clusters open in spring on stems that are left unpruned.

planting.note: A cold-hardy deciduous species; sow stratified seed in fall or plant in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun, giving this spreading shrub room to reach its mature width. Seed needs cold, moist stratification to break dormancy before it will sprout. White spring flowers are followed by red fruit and strong red-orange fall foliage.

planting.note: Formerly Stranvaesia; grown for lasting red berries. Plant in spring or fall.

directions: Plant this tall evergreen in well-drained soil where it has space to arch and spread. Stratified seed germinates slowly, so many gardeners start with nursery stock instead. White flowers in early summer are followed by heavy clusters of red berries that persist into winter.

planting.note: A hardy deciduous species with fine fall color; sow stratified seed in fall.

directions: Plant in moist but well-drained soil in sun to light shade, allowing room for its open, spreading crown. Cold stratification improves the slow, uneven germination of the seed. White flowers in late spring give way to red fruit, and the leaves color richly before dropping in autumn.

planting.note: A slender deciduous species; sow stratified seed in fall or plant in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun, where the airy branches show their small leaves and fruit to advantage. Seed germinates best after a cold, moist chilling period. Clusters of white flowers in spring are followed by bright red-orange berries in fall.

planting.note: A frost-tender species for mild and subtropical gardens; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained soil in a frost-free site where this large shrub or tree has room to grow. Fresh seed sprouts in warm, moist conditions and does not need chilling. White flower clusters appear in the warm season and are followed by small dark fruits.

planting.note: A tender-to-mild-climate species; plant in spring in a sheltered site.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and shelter from hard frost while young. Seed germinates in warm, moist conditions and establishes into a broad evergreen shrub. Flat clusters of white flowers open in summer and are followed by orange-red fruit.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring once the last frost has passed and the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant in a warm, sunny spot with well-drained soil, setting the root ball level with the surrounding grade. Water regularly the first season, then only during long dry spells since established shrubs are strongly drought tolerant. Prune lightly after the main flush to shape the plant; clustered single blooms in shades of pink open from late spring through autumn. All parts are highly poisonous, so wear gloves and keep clippings away from pets and children.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger has passed.

directions: Give this vigorous white variety plenty of room, as it grows taller and wider than most oleanders. Plant in full sun with fast-draining soil and stake young stems if you want an upright screen. Large single white flowers cover the shrub from early summer into fall; shear lightly after the first bloom flush to encourage repeat flowering.

planting.note: Set out in spring; among the more cold-tolerant selections.

directions: Plant this cold-hardier selection in full sun with well-drained soil where winters are mild. It regrows readily from the base if frost nips the top, so cut back damaged wood in spring. Soft pink single flowers appear in loose clusters from late spring through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered location.

directions: Choose a sunny, well-drained site and water through the first summer to establish the roots. This tough variety tolerates heat, salt spray, and brief cold snaps, resprouting from the base after a hard freeze. Deep red single flowers bloom continuously from late spring until frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring once soil warms.

directions: Set this hardy white form in full sun with sharp drainage and give it space to fill out. It withstands drought, heat, and coastal conditions once rooted in. Pure white single blooms open in tight clusters from late spring through autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; one of the more cold-resilient reds.

directions: Give this large, vigorous variety a sunny spot with room to reach its full size. Prized for reliable cold recovery, it resprouts strongly if frozen back and quickly rebuilds its canopy. Single cherry-red flowers with a light fragrance cover the shrub all summer long.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact grower for smaller spaces.

directions: This more compact, fine-textured variety fits smaller gardens and large containers. Plant in full sun with good drainage and pinch the tips of young plants to build density. Double salmon-pink flowers with a delicate fragrance appear from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: One of the few yellow oleanders, this old French variety wants full sun and well-drained soil. Prune after flowering to keep it shapely and remove any frost-damaged wood in spring. Double soft-yellow flowers open through the summer and fade to a paler cream with age.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant this pale-yellow variety in full sun with fast-draining soil for the best flower color. Water sparingly once established and prune lightly to maintain form. Single light-yellow flowers appear in airy clusters throughout the warm season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, warm bed.

directions: This softer-growing selection carries pale creamy-yellow single flowers and stays a bit smaller than the reds. Give it full sun and well-drained soil, watering deeply but infrequently once it takes hold. Blooms run from late spring through fall; shear after the first flush to prompt more buds.

planting.note: Plant in spring; more frost-sensitive than the full-size types.

directions: This naturally dwarf oleander is ideal for foundation beds, low hedges, and large pots. Plant in full sun with good drainage and avoid heavy pruning, which spoils its rounded shape. Small single pink flowers bloom nearly nonstop through the warm months; protect it from frost, as it is less hardy than standard varieties.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, protected spot.

directions: A compact companion to Petite Pink, this variety stays low and bushy for containers and small borders. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and water lightly once established. Delicate salmon single flowers appear over a long summer season; shelter the plant during cold snaps.

planting.note: Plant in spring; well suited to patio containers.

directions: This tidy, mounding variety was bred to stay compact without frequent pruning, making it a strong container plant. Give it full sun and well-drained soil, and feed lightly in spring for steady growth. Single pink flowers cover the plant from late spring to frost with almost no deadheading needed.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Valued for its deep, dark single red flowers, this variety stays somewhat more compact than the largest oleanders. Plant in full sun with good drainage and prune after blooming to keep it dense. Rich red flowers open through the summer against narrow deep-green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring; slower and smaller than green forms.

directions: Grown as much for its foliage as its flowers, this variety carries leaves edged in creamy yellow. Plant in full sun to keep the variegation bright and remove any all-green shoots that appear. Double salmon-pink flowers add to the display through summer; growth is slower than plain-leaved types.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: An old favorite with fully double, rose-pink flowers, this variety makes a large, showy shrub. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and prune after the main flush to control size. Heavy clusters of double blooms open from early summer well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; give it room to reach full size.

directions: This large, upright variety produces oversized double pink flowers on a tall frame. Site it in full sun with space to grow and train it as a screen or small multi-stemmed tree. Big fragrant double blooms appear all summer; prune in late winter to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a bright, warm site.

directions: This eye-catching variety pairs variegated cream-and-green leaves with single pink flowers. Grow it in full sun to hold the leaf color and pull off any reverted green stems. Flowers appear through summer above the striking foliage, making it useful even out of bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: A variegated selection combining gold-margined foliage with soft pink single flowers. Plant in full sun with good drainage and prune lightly to keep it bushy. The leaf color provides interest year-round while pink blooms carry through the summer months.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger.

directions: This distinctive variety bears unusually large red flowers with wavy, ruffled petal edges. Plant in full sun with fast-draining soil and water deeply but rarely once established. Big single blooms open from late spring through fall and stand out for their fringed look.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This variety produces soft mauve-pink single flowers on a robust upright plant. Give it full sun and well-drained soil and prune after flowering to keep its form. Clusters of pastel blooms cover the shrub through the summer and into early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a lower-growing selection.

directions: This lower, spreading variety carries light pink single flowers and stays more compact than the tall reds. Plant in full sun with good drainage and shear lightly to keep it dense. Pale pink blooms open in clusters throughout the warm season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: A dwarf red oleander that suits containers, low hedges, and tight borders. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and keep pruning minimal to preserve its compact habit. Small deep-red single flowers bloom over a long season; shield it from hard frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This variety offers bright, clear red single flowers on a medium, upright shrub. Site it in full sun with well-drained soil and water sparingly once rooted in. Vivid red blooms cover the plant from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: An old cultivar with double flowers in a rich purplish-red that fades softer with age. Plant in full sun with good drainage and prune after blooming to keep it shapely. Fragrant double blooms appear through the summer on a full, rounded shrub.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This classic French variety bears single dark-red flowers on a vigorous plant. Grow it in full sun with fast-draining soil and cut back frost-damaged wood in spring. Deep red blooms open continuously from late spring through fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny bed.

directions: This variety carries double salmon-pink flowers on a strong, upright shrub. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and water deeply but infrequently once established. Full double blooms appear from early summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: A tall, vigorous variety with large single rose-pink flowers. Give it full sun and room to grow, using it as a screen or specimen. Big single blooms cover the plant through the warm months; prune after flowering to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost has passed.

directions: This dependable variety produces clear pink single flowers on a well-branched shrub. Plant in full sun with good drainage and water lightly once established. Pink blooms appear in abundant clusters from late spring through fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: An old French cultivar bearing large, ruffled double flowers in soft salmon-pink. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and prune after the main flush to keep it full. Frilly double blooms open through the summer on a vigorous plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny location.

directions: This vigorous variety bears bright, single deep-red flowers over a long season. Plant in full sun with fast-draining soil and water only during extended dry spells. Rich red blooms cover the plant from early summer well into fall.

planting.note: Set plants out once nights stay warm and all frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at the same depth it grew in its pot, giving it room to sprawl or a support to scramble over. Pinch stem tips while young to build a fuller shrub and cut it back hard in early spring to keep it tidy. Expect airy clusters of sky-blue phlox-like flowers from late spring through autumn, heaviest in the summer heat.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors after the last frost when the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant this white-flowered form in a sunny, well-drained spot and water regularly until it is established. Prune in late winter to control its scrambling habit and encourage strong new flowering shoots. Pure white bloom clusters appear from early summer into fall and pair well with the blue species.

planting.note: Plant out in spring once frost has passed and soil is warm.

directions: Give this deeper-blue selection full sun and lean, fast-draining soil for the best flower color. It tolerates a touch more cold than the plain species but still needs protection where hard freezes occur. Cut back in early spring to renew growth and enjoy vivid cobalt-blue flowers through the warm months.

planting.note: Transplant after the last spring frost into warm ground.

directions: Plant this free-flowering selection where it gets full sun and can spill over a wall or fill a large container. Water moderately and feed lightly through the growing season, then prune hard in late winter. Rich blue flower heads carry on from late spring until frost.

planting.note: Plant in late spring when nights are reliably warm.

directions: Grow this tropical species in warm, humid conditions with rich, moist but well-drained soil. It sprawls loosely, so stake or prune to keep a shrubby shape. Long spikes of glowing rose-red to scarlet flowers appear mainly in the warm season; protect from any chill.

planting.note: Set out in late spring after all frost risk is gone.

directions: Plant in a warm, sheltered spot with well-drained soil and steady moisture during active growth. Its scrambling stems can be tied to a support or trimmed into a bushy mound. Slender spikes of white flowers with sticky calyces appear over a long warm season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, well-drained border.

directions: Give this compact, free-flowering selection full sun and sharp drainage for the strongest bloom. Prune close to the base in early spring to keep it dense and encourage new flowering wood. Deep blue flowers cover the plant from summer into autumn as the foliage reddens.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its golden leaves get good light.

directions: Plant this golden-leaved form in full sun so the bright yellow foliage keeps its color without scorching in the hottest afternoons. Cut back in early spring to renew growth and maintain a tidy mound. Sky-blue flowers set against the yellow leaves make a striking late-summer show.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered position.

directions: Plant this bushy, semi-evergreen shrub in full sun with well-drained soil and shelter from cold winds. Trim lightly after flowering or in early spring to keep a rounded form. Bright blue flowers appear from late summer into autumn as the leathery leaves take on purple-red tints.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a sunny bank or rock garden.

directions: Plant this low, twiggy shrub in full sun with sharp drainage, where it suits a rock garden or dry slope. Prune in early spring to remove any winter dieback and shape the plant. Clusters of blue flowers open in late summer and fall against reddening foliage.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant at the same depth it grew in the container and water deeply through the first summer to establish roots. Prune only lightly in late winter, removing crossing twigs and old seed heads rather than topping the trunks. Expect long panicles of crinkled bloom from midsummer into early fall on the current season's growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring so roots settle before the first bloom season.

directions: Give this tree-sized species room to develop its striking cinnamon-brown exfoliating bark, which becomes its main feature. Water regularly the first two years, then it tolerates drought well. Small white flower clusters appear in summer, but grow it mostly for the peeling trunks and mildew resistance it passes to its hybrids.

planting.note: Plant only where winters stay frost-free, in warm weather.

directions: This tropical tree needs a large frost-free site and steady moisture during the growing season. Plant in fertile, well-drained ground and protect young trees from cold snaps. Large lavender-to-purple flower clusters open through summer, followed by autumn foliage that turns red before dropping.

planting.note: Plant in spring with space for a multi-trunk small tree.

directions: Train this vigorous white-flowered hybrid to a few clean trunks to show off its cinnamon bark. It resists powdery mildew, so avoid crowding and let air move around it anyway. Pure white panicles carry from early summer to frost, longer than most crape myrtles.

planting.note: Give it full sun and room to reach small-tree size.

directions: Plant this fast-growing lavender hybrid where its 20-foot height fits, and thin to strong trunks as it matures. It is highly mildew resistant and drought tough once rooted in. Soft lilac flowers open over a long summer season and light gray-tan bark peels attractively.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the brightest color.

directions: Space this coral-pink hybrid so its rounded head has room to fill out on several trunks. Remove suckers and lower shoots each spring to keep a tidy vase form. Bright coral flowers appear through summer against dark green leaves that turn orange-red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; suits smaller yards as an upright small tree.

directions: This narrow, upright hybrid fits tighter spaces than the big tree types. Keep it in full sun and prune only to shape while dormant. Deep pink flowers cover the plant in summer and the foliage turns maroon-red before leaf drop.

planting.note: Plant in spring; good for a large shrub or low multi-stem.

directions: Grow this semi-dwarf as a rounded shrub or shape it into a small clump tree. It stays compact enough for foundation beds and resists mildew well. Fuchsia-red flowers bloom heavily through summer and leaves turn maroon in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its low, spreading form can arch.

directions: This semi-dwarf spreads wider than tall with gracefully weeping branch tips. Give it room to arch rather than shearing it into a ball. White flowers appear all summer and the fall foliage colors purple-red.

planting.note: Plant in spring; one of the more cold-hardy medium types.

directions: A dependable medium hybrid that grows as a rounded large shrub or small tree. It holds up in colder crape myrtle zones and blooms a long time. Medium lavender flowers open from midsummer to frost with good orange-red fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for strong coral color.

directions: This medium hybrid forms a dense rounded crown good for screening. Thin older wood to keep it open and mildew-free. Coral-pink flowers bloom over a long summer season with red-orange autumn foliage and light tan bark.

planting.note: Plant in spring as an upright medium multi-stem.

directions: An upright medium hybrid with an open branching habit that shows off its bark. Prune lightly in late winter and remove suckers each spring. Soft medium-lavender flowers open through summer above dark green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring where a tall upright tree form can develop.

directions: This tall, upright hybrid makes a striking small tree with dark cinnamon bark. Train it early to a few clean trunks and give it height clearance. Pale pink flowers open in summer and the branches take on an elegant vase shape with age.

planting.note: Plant in spring as an upright small tree.

directions: Grow this hybrid as an upright small tree with attractive chestnut-brown peeling bark. Keep the base clear of suckers to feature the trunks. Dark coral-pink flowers bloom through summer with orange-red fall foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a large upright specimen.

directions: A tall, vigorous hybrid prized for mahogany-red new bark that ages to lighter tan. Give it room and thin to strong trunks as it grows. Light magenta-lavender flowers open in summer and leaves turn russet-orange in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grown as much for bark as flowers.

directions: This upright medium hybrid has some of the finest cinnamon-orange bark in the group. Remove lower limbs over time to display the trunks. Light lavender flowers bloom in summer above leathery, mildew-resistant foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; broad-crowned small tree.

directions: Grow this broad, rounded hybrid as a shade-casting small tree with mottled bark. It is very mildew resistant and tolerates heat and drought. Clear pink flowers cover the crown in summer with bronze-orange fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its arching form can spread.

directions: This graceful hybrid has arching, pendulous branches and rich chestnut bark. Let the weeping form develop rather than pruning it upright. Clear light pink flowers open over a long summer and hold late into the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a rounded medium shrub.

directions: A dense, rounded medium hybrid that works well massed or as a single accent. Shape lightly in late winter to keep it full. Clear medium-pink flowers bloom heavily and reliably through the summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for deepest red.

directions: Grow this spreading medium hybrid for its true red flowers, which are uncommon in older crape myrtles. Full sun brings out the strongest color. Bright red panicles bloom through summer over a broad, rounded frame.

planting.note: Plant in spring as an upright medium tree.

directions: This strongly upright hybrid fits narrow spots and street plantings. Remove crossing branches to maintain its vertical form. Clear pink flowers open through summer above medium green foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; rounded medium form.

directions: A rounded medium hybrid grown for its consistent lavender-purple bloom. It resists mildew and holds up well in heat. Flowers appear from midsummer onward and the leaves turn yellow-orange in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; compact enough for foundation beds.

directions: This semi-dwarf stays compact and works well in smaller gardens and containers. It carries good mildew resistance and attractive chestnut bark. Medium pink flowers bloom over a long summer with maroon fall foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a rounded medium multi-stem.

directions: A medium, rounded hybrid noted for near-white to pale lavender bark. Feature the bark by removing lower limbs as the plant matures. Medium lavender flowers bloom through summer with orange-red autumn color.

planting.note: Plant in spring where a broad small tree can spread.

directions: This broad, horizontally branching hybrid makes a wide-crowned small tree. Give it room to spread rather than confining it. Dark pink flowers bloom through summer over leathery, dark green, mildew-resistant leaves.
